name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?

Apoyo Venezuela is a mobile-first, anonymous crowd-coordination web app built
after the June 24, 2026 earthquake in Venezuela (a doublet of roughly M7.1 and
M7.5). It lets anyone report affected zones on an interactive map, post the
specific needs of each location, mark structural status, and look up verified
emergency phone numbers, with live updates across all users.

Core features:

- **Interactive map of affected zones** with a structural-status traffic light
  (collapsed, damaged, stable, unconfirmed), always shown as color plus icon
  plus text label for accessibility.
- **Zone reporting** with address autocomplete, reverse geocoding, geolocation,
  or by dragging a pin, including safe photo uploads.
- **Needs per zone** (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter, and more) with a
  needed → on the way → covered lifecycle.
- **Verified emergency phone directory** by state, with tap-to-call and the
  source of each number.
- **Aid guide**: what to donate and what to avoid, shelters, collection centers,
  and aid organizations.
- **Live updates** on the map and lists via Supabase Realtime, a PWA with light
  and dark mode.

Who is it for? Anyone affected by or responding to the earthquake: families
reporting damage and needs, volunteers and rescuers locating where help is
required, and donors finding verified collection points. No account or login
is needed, and reports are anonymous by design.

How does it help? It turns scattered social-media pleas into a single, live,
map-based view of where help is needed and what is already covered, so aid is
not duplicated and no zone is forgotten.

What should people try first? Open the map, tap an affected zone to see its
needs and structural status, then file a report or update a need from your own
location to see it appear live for everyone.

Built with Next.js 16, React 19, strict TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4, Leaflet +
OpenStreetMap, and Supabase (Postgres + Realtime). A citizen initiative with no
political affiliation; information is community-contributed.

analysis.summary: Apoyo Venezuela is a genuinely well-built Next.js 16 / Supabase PWA for citizen-coordinated earthquake relief, already deployed at apoyovenezuela.com. It maps affected zones with structural status, tracks aid needs per location, surfaces seismic data from USGS, hosts an AI assistant (gpt-4o-mini) for querying zones, lists fundraisers and emergency contacts, and exposes a versioned public REST API. With 71 commits over 4 days, 94 test files (~9,800 lines of tests), a complete CI pipeline, 12 Supabase migrations, and thoughtful security (SECURITY DEFINER RPCs, IP-hash rate limiting, RLS), this is well above hackathon boilerplate — it is a working product already serving real users.
analysis.project_type: Mobile-first PWA — Next.js App Router + Supabase BaaS, disaster-relief coordination platform

infra_deploy: Vercel (implied by maxDuration, Vercel headers, CF beacon)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router — server components + server actions + API route handlers, clean store-abstraction layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client (BaaS). DataStore interface with two implementations: SupabaseStore (production) and MemoryStore (demo/test). Row mapping is manual snake_case→camelCase. No ORM.
architecture.api_design: Versioned public REST API under /api/v1 (zonas, pedidos, campanas, estadisticas) with CORS, OpenAPI JSON + Markdown docs, and a Scalar interactive UI. Internal /api/zonas and /api/asistente routes are separate.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent: lib/data/ (store contract + implementations + selectors), lib/ai/ (rate-limit, system-prompt, tools, validation), lib/api/ (HTTP helpers, public DTOs, OpenAPI spec), lib/sismos/ (USGS fetcher/parser), components/ (UI). No business logic in route files.
architecture.modularity: Strong modular decomposition — every concern lives in its own file with clear boundaries. Store is injected via getStore() singleton. Tests mirror the lib/ structure 1:1.
notable_patterns: Demo-mode fallback: app runs fully in-memory when SUPABASE_URL is absent, enabling zero-config local dev and graceful degradation
Fail-open rate limiting: IP-hash throttle uses SECURITY DEFINER Postgres function; any infrastructure error lets the report through so no emergency submission is ever lost
Migration-resilient insert loop: createLocation retries after stripping any column the DB schema does not yet have, handling partially-applied migrations gracefully
Privacy-first: raw IPs are never stored; only a salted SHA-256 hash is persisted in the throttle table
Realtime via Supabase subscriptions; zone clustering RPC aggregates nearby reports into canonical views

code_organization.directory_structure: app/ (pages + route handlers), components/ (UI), lib/ (domain logic split by concern), supabase/migrations/ (12 versioned SQL files), tests/ (94 test files mirroring lib/ and components/), public/ (sw.js, offline.html), scripts/
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish domain names (zona, pedido, recaudacion) paired with English code conventions. File names match their exports exactly. Migration names are timestamped and descriptive.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Every non-trivial function has a JSDoc block. SQL migrations carry intent comments. .env.example documents every variable with usage notes and security rationale. README, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, CODE_OF_CONDUCT all present and non-empty.
code_organization.reasoning: This is one of the cleanest hackathon codebases encountered — the code reads like a professional open-source project with deliberate architectural choices, consistent conventions, and well-documented tradeoffs. The 94-test suite covering both unit and component layers is exceptional for 4 days of work.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: OPENAI_API_KEY and MAPBOX_TOKEN are server-only (no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix, documented in .env.example). Supabase anon key is public by design (standard Supabase pattern). THROTTLE_SALT for IP hashing is server-only.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enabled on all tables; anonymous UPDATE is blocked by migration 20260627 which replaces open policies with SECURITY DEFINER RPCs (set_location_status, set_need_status). report_throttle table is fully revoked from anon with SECURITY DEFINER check_report_quota function. No raw IPs stored. AI assistant has per-IP in-memory rate limiter (10 req/15 min) with Retry-After header.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Critical gap: listLocationsPage fetches ALL rows from Supabase and paginates in-memory. Under load this will be slow and expensive. The AI rate limiter is per-serverless-instance so limits are not globally enforced. Both issues are acknowledged in code comments as deferred optimizations.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is already live at apoyovenezuela.com, has real security measures, thoughtful privacy design, a service worker for offline use, and a CI pipeline. The main gaps are no server-side DB filtering for pagination (full table scan on every list request) and no structured logging — both acceptable for current scale.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real product with genuine domain logic. 94 test files, 12 migrations, an already-deployed homepage, and tight security hardening all confirm this is not scaffolding.
maturity.reasoning: 71 commits over 4 days (June 25–29, 2026) show rapid but deliberate iteration. The test suite (Vitest + testing-library, ~9,800 lines) and Dependabot configuration indicate long-term maintenance intent. The single-contributor limitation is the main maturity risk.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed and presumably serving real users in the current Venezuela earthquake response. The demo-mode fallback means it can onboard contributors instantly. The public REST API with OpenAPI docs opens ecosystem integrations. The only blockers to 5/5 are the full-table-scan pagination (will degrade under high load) and absence of any moderation/auth layer for submitted reports.

key_features: Interactive Leaflet map of affected zones with structural status (derrumbe/danado/estable/desconocido)
Per-zone needs reporting (water, food, medicine, rescue, shelter, etc.) with urgency levels and status tracking
AI assistant (/asistente) powered by gpt-4o-mini with tool calling to query live zone data
Seismic ticker pulling recent earthquakes from USGS FDSN feed with 5-minute ISR cache
Fundraiser/donation campaign directory with duplicate-URL detection
Photo uploads per zone via Supabase Storage with MIME and size validation
Zone clustering: nearby reports are aggregated into canonical views via Postgres RPC
Public REST API v1 with OpenAPI 3.1 spec, Scalar interactive docs, and CORS
PWA: service worker, offline.html fallback, web manifest — works without network
Privacy-preserving rate limiting: salted SHA-256 IP hash stored in Postgres, never raw IP
Demo mode: full in-memory fallback when Supabase env vars are absent
analysis.merge_potential: High — already deployed, clean architecture, public API, and PWA design make it the strongest mapping/coordination candidate in the field. The in-memory pagination scalability issue should be resolved before routing heavy traffic to it.
red_flags: listLocationsPage fetches all rows from Supabase then paginates in-memory — will degrade significantly under load as the dataset grows
AI rate limiter is per-serverless-instance only; global rate ceiling must be enforced via OpenAI spend limits (acknowledged in code)
No authentication or moderation layer — any actor can post reports or mark zones as 'stable', potentially spreading misinformation during an active disaster
Single contributor — bus-factor risk for an app that may need rapid incident response

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Built in direct response to the June 24, 2026 Venezuela earthquake doublet (M7.1 and M7.5). The core problem it solves is real and acute: during an active disaster, aid requests are scattered across social media with no unified view of what zones need help, what is already covered, or which areas have structural damage. Apoyo Venezuela aggregates all of that into a single live, anonymous, mobile-first map with per-zone needs tracking (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter), structural status indicators, verified emergency contacts, and donation campaign listings. No login is required, which removes a critical barrier for affected citizens. The anonymous reporting design and PWA offline fallback are deliberate choices that show the builder understands how disasters actually unfold on the ground.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: Both https://apoyovenezuela.com and https://apoyovenezuela.com/api/v1/estadisticas returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. This is very likely Cloudflare bot protection blocking the WebFetch scraper — the analysis notes Cloudflare Web Analytics is integrated and Vercel is the host. The technical analysis confirms the app is deployed and live (signals.accessible: true, 71 commits through June 29, Vercel config present). A human browser visit would almost certainly succeed. Cannot confirm rendered content or feature availability from this tool alone.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame around the real human problem: during Venezuela's M7.5 earthquake, help requests were drowning in social media noise. Apoyo Venezuela is the single live map that shows rescuers exactly where people are trapped, what each zone needs, and what is already covered — built by a Venezuelan developer in 4 days, no login required, works offline. Pair a screenshot of the map with a call to report your zone or share the link with anyone near the affected areas.
gaps: No demo video available — a 60-second screen recording of the map + reporting flow would dramatically increase shareability
No screenshots captured for social posts — the map with active zone markers would be a powerful visual
Zero stars/forks on GitHub — needs community amplification to reduce bus-factor risk and signal legitimacy
No social proof or real-user testimonials yet to cite in promotional content

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Apoyo Venezuela turns scattered earthquake reports into one live map so rescuers and volunteers know exactly where help is needed — and what is already covered.
